topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with a particularly grating monotony that afternoon. I was meticulously organizing files, a task that usually soothed my frayed nerves, but today, it felt like sandpaper against my skin. Then I saw the spreadsheet. A quick glance, a moment of frozen breath, and the numbers swam before my eyes. Sarah, the new intern, was earning significantly more than me. A dull ache settled low in my chest, a heavy, unfamiliar weight. I traced the line item with my finger, the plastic cool beneath my touch. It wasn't anger that rose, just a profound, quiet sadness, as if a small, cherished part of my own effort had just been measured against something unattainable. I quickly minimized the window, my hands trembling slightly, and forced a smile that felt brittle on my face.

[story 2]

I leaned back in my chair, the cheap vinyl groaning in protest. The air in the cubicle felt thick, suffocating. When I finally looked at the comparison, the reality hit me with a strange, hollow thud. Liam, who had been diligently working on the project for months, was making substantially more than I was. I stared at the screen, my gaze lingering on his name. A strange wetness pricked at the corners of my eyes, and I blinked rapidly, trying to scrub the feeling away. It was a deep ache, the kind that settles in your bones when you realize a path you were walking isn't the one you were meant to tread. I picked up my pen, the plastic feeling suddenly foreign, and started doodling elaborate, meaningless patterns on my notepad, seeking refuge in the trivial.

[story 3]

It happened during the monthly payroll review. I was reviewing my own statement, a routine check, when I saw the disparity glaringly obvious. Chloe, who had been diligently shadowing me, earned a considerable premium. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I felt a sudden, sharp longing, a wistful yearning for a different kind of recognition, a different kind of financial security. I folded the paper slowly, deliberately, the sound loud in the otherwise quiet office. My shoulders slumped, a visible surrender to the imbalance. I looked out the window at the cityscape, feeling small and suddenly very alone in my own small corner of the building.
